design_philosophy.core_loop: Pilot submarine to destroy alien facilities, rescue captured humans, manage fuel supply, transport survivors to island checkpoints for resupply and power-ups
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Dual resource management—fuel depletion every 60 seconds creates constant pressure; human cargo capacity (max 9) forces strategic evacuation decisions; enemy avoidance while maintaining forward momentum
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Reluctant hero submarine commander executing precision military strikes against invading alien forces; rescuing humanity from oceanic conquest
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Fuel as a hard timer mechanic (failure = instant death) creates genuine survival pressure distinct from typical shoot-em-ups. Rescue mechanic subordinates combat to logistics—success measured by survivors delivered, not enemies defeated. Aquatic setting with ice-ball facility destruction provides thematic coherence absent in standard horizontal shooters.

difficulty.lives_system: Loss of life when fuel depletes (60-second timer per segment); additional losses from enemy contact
difficulty.checkpointing: Island checkpoints function as mandatory stops where rescued humans are discharged, fuel replenished, and power-ups granted; progression gated by successful evacuations
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player controls pacing of fuel consumption through movement speed and combat engagement; can manage risk by evacuating fewer survivors per trip (shorter fuel distance) or pushing capacity (longer risk windows); optional mermaid collision for hidden bonus message introduces risk-reward choice

progression.upgrade_system: Power-ups granted upon island discharge of rescued survivors; progressive difficulty across 8 levels with three distinct loop cycles
progression.unlock_mechanism: Completion of level 7 triggers ending sequence (Japanese version: base destruction + mermaid collision unlock regional-specific messages); U.S. version gates final story beat behind all three loops + full base destruction
replayability_hooks: Hidden mermaid collision bonus message (Japanese version) accessible on any loop
Regional narrative variation between JP and U.S. versions creates replay incentive for story completionists
Base destruction mechanic ties ending outcome to player performance quality

world_building_techniques: Environmental setup: polar ice caps melted by Neptune aliens, continents submerged, Earth Defense Army appeals to pirate protagonist
Character motivation subversion: reluctant hero Narikeen laughs at initial plea but agrees to fight; establishes morally ambiguous protagonist
Alien motivation clarity: Neptunians/Ottoto aliens explicitly seeking 'man-ham livestock' as nutritional resource; invasion framed as resource extraction
Regional story divergence: Japanese version identifies distress origin as Pentagon; U.S. version alters threat attribution and surrender conditions
Visual narrative: pink submarine Sqoon, ice-ball bombs, human cargo visual indicators, alien base facilities as destructible story beats

faction_indicators: Protagonist Narikeen (pirate, morally ambiguous, reluctant participant)
danger_signals: Polar ice cap destruction as planetary-scale threat indicator
Submersion of continents signals existential stakes
Fuel depletion mechanic frames resource scarcity as active danger
Enemy contact = instant life loss (permadeath until continue), emphasizing vulnerability
environmental_storytelling: Underwater setting visually communicates catastrophic climate change
Alien facilities serve as concrete targets for narrative agency
Island checkpoints represent safe havens and human civilization refuges
Power-up distribution upon rescue suggests military supply chain metaphor

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor) - Hidden developer credits, unused graphics, regional differences (introduction/ending text variants)
wikipedia - Plot summary, gameplay mechanics, developer/publisher attribution, release dates
